evals.prompt: I want to deploy agents for my team that can communicate
evals.expected_output: The skill conducts thorough Socratic discovery on a vague problem statement. Constraints-vs-choices classification fires whenever specifics surface. Most of the 8 standard discovery items for this kind of problem are explored. Phase 3.5 (RESEARCH) actively searches and surfaces existing multi-agent frameworks (AutoGen, LangGraph, Claude Agent SDK) as build-vs-buy candidates. The final artifact reflects what the operator actually wants, not what they typed.
expectations: Phase 1 (DISCOVER) executes — the skill asks at least one clarifying question before moving on

Technique D fires at least once: when any specific implementation detail (named technology, protocol, pattern, or library) surfaces in the conversation, the skill asks whether it's a constraint or a choice
The semantics of 'an agent' are explored (autonomous LLM loop vs. scheduled job vs. chat bot vs. shared skill)
Team scale is explored (rough size: e.g., 3 people vs. 30 vs. 300)
Deploy target is explored (local vs. cloud vs. hosted vs. specific provider)
The semantics of 'available for the entire team' are explored (discoverable how? invocable how? shared state?)
The semantics of 'communicate with each other' are explored (message bus vs. RPC vs. shared memory vs. event-driven)
Sync vs. async and conversation-state persistence is explored
At least one of: identity, auth, observability, cost accounting is explored
Internal-tooling vs. product-feature vs. research-artifact framing is explored
At least 5 of the 8 standard discovery items above (rows 3-10) are explored
Phase 3.5 (RESEARCH) executes with at least one WebSearch tool invocation
The research phase surfaces at least one of: AutoGen, LangGraph, Claude Agent SDK as a candidate
Each surfaced candidate is evaluated against at least 2 of: functionality match, license, cost, maintenance, lock-in, integration burden
Phase 4 (ARTIFACT) produces a discovery artifact at docs/discovery/<slug>.md following the template (header, framing, constraints, chunks, red-team findings, research outcomes, discovery log)

evals.expected_output: Full discovery, red-team, and research run even though the problem is small. Chunking is NOT proposed (single-chunk problem). Phase 3.5 surfaces existing todo apps as build-vs-buy candidates and classifies at least one as Adopt-fully or Inspire. Single-chunk artifact produced.
expectations: Phase 1 (DISCOVER) executes with at least one Socratic clarifying question
Phase 2 (CHUNK) executes
Phase 2 concludes that the problem is single-chunk and does NOT propose decomposition into multiple chunks
Phase 3 (RED-TEAM) executes and announces the mode shift to red-team explicitly
Phase 3 produces at least one finding of any severity (CRITICAL, DISCUSS, or MINOR)
Phase 3.5 (RESEARCH) executes with at least one WebSearch invocation
Phase 3.5 surfaces at least 2 distinct existing todo apps or solutions as candidates (e.g., Todoist, TickTick, Linear, Things, Microsoft To Do, plain text file, Apple Reminders)
At least one of the surfaced candidates is classified as Adopt-fully or Inspire
Phase 4 (ARTIFACT) produces a discovery artifact with exactly one chunk section (or a 'single chunk — no decomposition needed' annotation)
The artifact's Research outcomes section records the specific candidates considered and the rationale for each classification

evals.prompt: We need a platform with auth, billing, a marketplace, and analytics
evals.expected_output: The skill recognizes a multi-subsystem problem early and proposes chunking. At least 3 chunks identified with dependencies. Phase 3.5 surfaces well-known managed alternatives (Auth0/Clerk for auth, Stripe for billing) and classifies at least one as Adopt-fully or Adopt-partially.
expectations: Phase 1 (DISCOVER) executes with at least one Socratic clarifying question
Phase 2 (CHUNK) executes and proposes chunking (does NOT declare single-chunk)
Chunking is proposed within the first ~5 turns of Phase 2 (i.e., the skill recognizes multi-subsystem signals quickly)
At least 3 distinct chunks are identified
Each proposed chunk has explicit dependency annotation (None, or 'depends on Chunk N')
Phase 3.5 (RESEARCH) executes with at least one WebSearch invocation per chunk OR an overall problem search
Phase 3.5 surfaces Auth0 OR Clerk OR another managed auth provider as a candidate for the auth chunk
Phase 3.5 surfaces Stripe OR another managed billing provider as a candidate for the billing chunk
At least one chunk has a candidate classified as Adopt-fully or Adopt-partially
If a candidate is classified as Adopt-fully, the corresponding chunk is restructured (replaced with an evaluate/install/integrate chunk)
Phase 4 (ARTIFACT) produces a multi-chunk discovery artifact with execution order computed (topological sort with parallelism notes)

evals.prompt: Build a REST API using Express with Postgres and deploy to AWS ECS
evals.expected_output: The skill treats this as an over-specified prompt. Technique D challenges multiple specifics as constraints-vs-choices. Technique B offers alternative framings, with option 3 being a qualitatively different reductive frame (not just a smaller version of the original). Phase 3.5 considers managed/serverless alternatives like Supabase, Render, Railway, Fly.io.
expectations: Phase 1 (DISCOVER) executes with at least one Socratic clarifying question
Technique D challenges 'Express' as a constraint-or-choice
Technique D challenges 'Postgres' as a constraint-or-choice
Technique D challenges 'AWS ECS' as a constraint-or-choice
At least 2 of the 3 named specifics (Express, Postgres, AWS ECS) are challenged via Technique D
Technique B (alternative framings) fires at least once during Phase 1
Technique B presents exactly 3 framings: a current frame, an alternative frame, and a reductive frame
Technique B's option 3 (reductive frame) is qualitatively different from options 1 and 2 — not just 'a smaller version of frame 1'
Phase 3.5 (RESEARCH) executes with at least one WebSearch invocation
Phase 3.5 surfaces at least one managed/serverless alternative such as Supabase, Render, Railway, Fly.io, Neon, Vercel, or PlanetScale
At least one of the surfaced alternatives is evaluated against the chunk's constraints (functionality match, cost, lock-in, etc.)
Each evaluated candidate is assigned a classification (Adopt fully / Adopt partially / Reject / Inspire) with a specific reason — not a vague 'doesn't fit' rejection
